title: Kaplan vs. Chinchilla Scaling Laws
concept: Kaplan's early scaling laws suggested prioritizing parameter size over data size; the Chinchilla scaling laws corrected this, showing parameters and tokens should scale equally.

exercises.prompt: What do scaling laws in AI help researchers do?

options.text: Predict how AI performance improves with more compute, data, and parameters
Dictate the legal rules for using AI in public schools
Measure the physical dimensions of the computers running the AI

exercises.explain: Scaling laws act as a recipe book, predicting how much better an AI will perform as we give it more computer power, parameters, and data.

cards.title: Size vs. Knowledge
cards.body: Is it always better to build a bigger brain, or do you just need to read more books? In the race to build super-smart AI, researchers debated this exact question. Let's look at the scaling laws that changed how we train AI forever!

cards.title: What are Scaling Laws?
cards.body: Scaling laws are mathematical guides that predict how smart an AI will get as we increase compute power, model size (parameters), and dataset size (tokens). Kaplan laws focused heavily on making models bigger, while Chinchilla laws showed that data must grow just as fast as the model's size.

cards.title: The Cookie Baker Analogy
cards.body: Imagine baking giant cookies. Kaplan laws suggested that to make the best cookie, you should just build a massive oven (huge model parameters), even if you only have a small bowl of cookie dough (little data). Chinchilla laws corrected this, explaining that to bake the perfect cookie, your oven size and your amount of dough must grow together in perfect harmony.

cards.title: Chinchilla vs. GPT-3
cards.body: When OpenAI built GPT-3, they followed Kaplan's ideas, creating a massive model trained on a relatively small dataset. Later, DeepMind trained Chinchilla, a model 4 times smaller than GPT-3 but trained on 4 times more data. Chinchilla easily outperformed GPT-3, proving that more data makes smaller models smarter!

cards.title: Myth: Bigger is Always Better
cards.body: Many people believed that to get a smarter AI, you simply had to make the model parameters as large as possible. In reality, a giant model trained on too little data is 'undertrained' and wasteful. Chinchilla proved that data size is just as important as model size.

cards.title: Scaling Law Showdown
cards.body: A comparison of how Kaplan and Chinchilla scaling laws approach model building.

left.text: Builds giant models with massive parameters, even if data is limited.

right.text: Balances model parameters and data tokens in equal proportions.

exercises.prompt: What did Kaplan's early scaling laws suggest we should prioritize?

options.text: Prioritize model parameters over data size
Prioritize data size over model parameters

exercises.explain: Kaplan's early scaling laws suggested that model size (parameters) was more important to scale up than the amount of training data.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Under Kaplan's laws, models were often built so large that they ended up undertrained due to a lack of data.
exercises.explain: Chinchilla laws proved that many early models (like GPT-3) were actually undertrained because they had too many parameters relative to the amount of data they were given.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the Chinchilla scaling rule.
exercises.sentence: According to Chinchilla scaling laws, we should scale model parameters and training data tokens <mask>.
exercises.explain: Chinchilla scaling laws showed that to get the most out of our computers, we must scale model parameters and data tokens equally.

exercises.prompt: Identify the AI development plan that correctly follows the Chinchilla scaling laws:

options.text: AI Model A: Doubles its parameter count and doubles its training data.
AI Model B: Quadruples its parameter count but keeps the same small dataset.
AI Model C: Shrinks its parameters to half while doubling the dataset.

exercises.explain: AI Model A is the optimal choice because it scales both parameters and data tokens together, which aligns with Chinchilla scaling laws. Model B ignores data scaling, and Model C scales data while shrinking the model.

exercises.prompt: You have a fixed budget to train a new AI model and want to achieve the absolute best performance based on Chinchilla scaling laws. What is your best strategy?

options.text: Make the model 10 times larger while keeping the dataset size the same
Scale both the model parameters and the number of training tokens in equal proportion
Only increase the training tokens and shrink the model parameters to save space
Ignore dataset size and focus exclusively on training for more epochs

exercises.explain: Chinchilla scaling laws proved that for optimal performance under a fixed budget, we should scale the model size (parameters) and the training dataset size (tokens) in equal proportion. Over-focusing on parameters alone creates a giant, undertrained model.
